paper_title: What Will It Take for Jordan to Grow?
research_answer: Jordan's total exports collapsed dramatically from 56% of GDP in 2008 to just 34% of GDP in 2016 — a decline of 22 percentage points over eight years. This was driven by a combination of shocks including the Global Financial Crisis, regional instability, and the loss of key trade corridors through Syria and Iraq. Goods exports never recovered as a share of GDP after the GFC, continuing to decline throughout the decade. However, a partial recovery began after 2016, led primarily by tourism (services exports), which had fallen mid-decade but began growing again. This recovery was then cut short by COVID-19's devastating impact on global travel.
supporting_quotes: Exports collapsed from 56% of GDP in 2008 to 34% of GDP in 2016, but export recovery had begun, led by tourism, as exports climbed gradually each of the next three years.
Exports of goods never recovered as a share of GDP after the GFC and in fact continued to decline throughout the decade.
Service exports fell as a share of GDP in the middle of the last decade and had begun to grow until COVID-19 introduced an unprecedented shock to global travel and tourism.
